dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a 72-year-old male with chest pain, fever, and chills at a medical office.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT. What happened today that brought you to call 911?
I've had left-sided chest pain all morning. It started when I woke up and felt like something was torn or pulled. I also have chills and a fever.
Can you tell me where we are?
We're in my doctor's office in Richmond.
Good. Do you know what time or what day it is?
I think it's Thursday afternoon. I was in for a routine visit when I suddenly got chest pain and felt feverish with chills.
Frank, can you open your eyes and follow my light?
Yeah, they're wide open right now.
You're AAOx4. Can you move your arms and legs for me?
I can move them just fine.
Your airway is clear. Are you having trouble breathing right now?
I can breathe, but the pain makes it hard at times.
Your skin looks normal, and your pulse is strong and regular.
Can you describe any past medical issues or surgeries?
I've got a lot of medical issues-cardiomyopathy, sleep apnea, bipolar disorder, kidney disease, skin cancer, hepatitis C, and tuberculosis.
What medications are you taking?
Trospium, Cholecalciferol, Tamsulosin, Metoprolol, Carbamazepine, and Haloperidol. I used to take Lithium, Chlorpromazine, Latuda, and Lamictal, but not currently.
Tell me more about your chest pain. How would you rate its severity?

Yes, it's a 9/10 sharp pain on the left side, and it's pleuritic-worse when I breathe in. It started today in the morning when I got out of bed. The doctor mentioned my wound from the pacer is slightly red, but it doesn't smell bad or leak.
Do you have known allergies?
No, I don't have allergies listed.
Is anything making the pain better or worse?
Nope, nothing makes it better or worse.
Your head and neck appear fine, no signs of pain or trauma either. I'll check your pupils now.
Pupils are equal and reactive. I'm going to the next step-listening to your lungs now.
I am listening to your lungs now. They're clear and you're not in respiratory distress with a rate of 16 breaths per minute.
Pupils are equal and reactive.
You're alert and oriented. Your GCS is 15.
Your first blood pressure is 178 over 82.
Your respiratory rate is 16.
Your pulse is 76.
You're at 95% oxygen saturation on room air.
Our glucose monitor isn't working. We'll continue monitoring.
I'm placing a 4-lead monitor now. The EKG result shows a paced rhythm.
A 12-lead EKG has been performed, and it confirms the paced rhythm with no acute changes detected.
I'll be placing an IV in your left forearm. I'll use a 20-gauge needle and start it with normal saline.
I'm going to give you 324 mg of aspirin. This will help with the inflammation. Are you okay with this?
Yes, go ahead.
We will flush the IV line with normal saline to ensure it remains functional.
At the start, your BP was 178 over 82, and the heart rate was 76. We also gave you 324 mg of ASA PO.
Your blood pressure has dropped to 148 over 78, and your oxygen saturation is down to 94%. The rest is stable.
Now your blood pressure is at 141 over 81, with oxygen saturation at 92%.
72-year-old male with a call type of Heart Problems/AICD. He has left-sided chest pain, is AAOx4, and shows no signs of JVD or respiratory distress.

His current medical problems include cardiomyopathy, sleep apnea, bipolar disorder, obesity, hepatitis C, kidney disease, skin cancer, and tuberculosis. He also has a ventricular pacer with a wound that appears slightly red.

Vital signs: BP started at 178/82, followed by 148/78 and 141/81. Pulse remained at 76. Respiratory rate at 16 each time. Oxygen saturation: 95, 94, and 92. All interventions, including 324 mg ASA PO, were provided and documented.
Transport readiness is confirmed. Equipment and patient are both stable and ready.
We are taking the patient to VCU Health System based on both his and his doctor's request.
Patient has been secured to the stretcher and is being transported in a comfortable position.
Your chest pain is still at a 9/10, but no change in location or radiation.
Pain remains sharp and pleuritic with no triggering factors reported.
No new symptoms reported. No change in fever or chills.
BP is 141/81, heart rate 76, respiratory rate 16. SPO2 is still at 92%.
Patient remains alert and oriented, GCS 15.
Pupils remain reactive and equal with no signs of neurological compromise.
Skin looks normal, warm, and dry with no rashes or bite marks noted.
Abdomen is soft and non-tender with normal bowel sounds and no palpable masses.
No edema, deformity, or angulation in extremities. PMS is intact bilaterally.
Head shape and contour are normal with no trauma or pressure points.
Neck appears soft and pliable with no signs of pain or rigidity.
Rechecking 12-lead EKG: still shows a paced rhythm with no acute changes.
I am going to listen to your heart and lungs again.
Heart sounds are normal, no S3 or murmur noted.
Bilateral breath sounds are clear, no wheezes or crackles heard.
Ears appear normal, no drainage or obstruction.
Mouth is free of trauma or unusual findings.
Throat appears clear, no signs of difficulty swallowing or masses.
You mentioned being 72-does that feel accurate to you?
You are a male patient, correct?
Yes, I take Trospium, Cholecalciferol, Tamsulosin, Metoprolol, Carbamazepine, and Haloperidol.
Describe the events that led you to call 911 today?
I had the chest pain this morning when I got out of bed. I called 911 when it became very sharp and I went to my doctor's office for evaluation.
When exactly did the pain start for you?
It started first thing when I woke up today.
Does anything make the pain worse?
Breathing in makes it worse-very pleuritic.
How would you describe the pain in words?
It's sharp and piercing on the left side.
Does it spread anywhere else?
No, it stays localized to the left chest.
On a scale from one to ten, how bad is the pain?
It is a nine out of ten.
So it has been going on for a few hours now?
Yeah, started this morning when I woke up.
Are you still alert and can you answer a yes/no question now?
Yes, I'm doing fine.
Is it easier to breathe than when we first started?
No, same as before.
Your O2 is still coming in at 92 percent-no increase.
BP is still approximately 141 over 81.
Lungs remain clear with no wheezing or decreased air movement noted.
We are unable to get a glucose reading at this time using the available equipment.
We will maintain IV access and continue flushing with normal saline.
Patient remains in good condition during transport. All interventions have been documented.
Blood pressure readings, EKG results, IV placement, and administration of 324 mg ASA PO have been noted.
Patient will be arriving at VCU with no incident reported in route.
All equipment is on board and ready for hospital arrival.
We're in position with all documents and patient ready.
Patient is alert, stable, with an established IV and 324 mg ASA administered.
No known allergies have been reported by the patient.
The patient has signed for himself and agreed to all administered care.
Delayed transport due to a third-party accident that we stopped to help.
Final suspected diagnosis includes non-cardiac chest pain and fever.
Patient is moving to a bed by his own power as reported by the narrative.
Transfer completed to hospital staff in ER bed following all procedures.
Final check on all procedures and interventions done.
No additional procedures initiated during transport.
Patient safely delivered with no complications during transport.
Final oxygen saturation is still at 92%.
Final BP: 141/81, still within acceptable range.
All patient care has been transferred to the emergency staff.
Documents and protocols have been passed on accordingly.
Patient did well during the journey, and no additional medical events occurred.
Summary shared with receiving nurse for care planning.
All sets of vitals are recorded per required protocols.
Patient care equipment has been properly secured and cleaned.
Continued attention ensured during transport as per field impression.
Per the field impression, no further interventions were required post handoff.
